title: I Investigated America’s Unhealthiest Restaurant Meals

chunks.title: Introduction to Chain Restaurant Eating
chunks.summary: The video opens with a stark statistic about the high number of Americans dining at chain restaurants daily. It sets the stage for a discussion on the unhealthy nature of these foods, highlighting the skyrocketing rates of obesity and chronic illnesses linked to dietary choices.
chunks.transcript: 85 million Americans eat at a chain restaurant in the United States every single day. That's a fourth of the US population. In this video, I'm going to visit three of the most popular chain restaurants in the United States. I'm going to order their food. I'm going to look at the menu and I'm going to expose things on the menu that you don't even know are there that are kind of crazy. But also, at every one of these chain restaurants, I'm going to show you what I would order. See if I can get healthier options in what could be bad scenarios. As Americans, we're incredibly unhealthy. 70 plus% of us are obese and overweight. Chronic illness is skyrocketing. Autoimmune conditions are skyrocketing. Dementia is skyrocketing. Cancers are skyrocketing. And it's primarily because of the food we eat. We're going to go to a breakfast place, a common lunch place and one of the most popular dinner places in the country. And at every one of these places, we're

chunks.summary: The host visits IHOP, discussing the unhealthy components of their menu items, such as the use of pancake batter in omelets and the presence of high fructose corn syrup in syrups. He emphasizes the need for awareness about ingredients and healthier preparation options.

chunks.title: Analysis of In-N-Out
chunks.summary: Next, the host arrives at In-N-Out and critiques the convenience of fast food, suggesting it's a scam. He discusses the unhealthy components of their menu such as seed oils used for frying and the hidden ingredients in sauces.
chunks.transcript: here at In-N-Out. I just finished up my uh my breakfast at IHOP maybe 30 minutes ago, so I'm hungry again. I'm having a sugar crash, so I'm going to need to come over here and get some like soda or something because my my blood sugar is really crashing. Before I even go into In-N-Out, though, I want to point out the drive-thru. Look at the size of this line, guys. It goes all the way around and I think it loops behind the In-N-Out and then it comes out over here. 2:45 in the afternoon on a Monday outside of Houston, Texas, and there's probably 40 people in the drive-thru at In-N-Out. Fast [music] food is a scam. It's not cheaper. You can make what's at In-N-Out easily at your home for way cheaper than it's going to cost you here. You can make baked potatoes, you can make a burger, you can put some tomatoes on it for much cheaper. And you think about how long you sit in that drive-thru, it's not even really faster. What's the point? It's convenience. We're lazy. That's the problem, guys. Because if you go to the grocery store, if you batch your shopping, and then you cook for yourself, you're going to take maybe 20 or 30 minutes to make your lunch and eat it. How long did it take you to drive from your work to In-N-Out? How long did you sit in that drive-thru line? How long do you have to wait over here in your car to eat the food? Then you have to drive back to your work. So, here's the deal. If you actually want to be healthy, you have to rebel against convenience. You have to rebel against laziness. That's the problem here. Anyway, let's go into In-N-Out and see what the best thing I can get on the menu is. And the worst thing on the menu. I'm going to get a a chocolate shake and I'm going to get a French fries. What are the French fries cooked in? Is it It's peanut oil or what kind of oil? >> Sunflower oil. >> Sunflower oil. Okay. And then I'm going to get a double double. And then do you guys have a Flying Dutchman? Like just the just the the patties and the cheese. No, no buns. >> Okay. So, I'll get one of those, too. Do you know how often you guys change the fryer oil? >> I'm not sure. >> I can be curious. >> Yeah. How often we change it? >> So, we change 100% of it every Wednesday morning. >> Otherwise, we kind of move it up as we use it throughout the day and then refill uh the following morning. So you're looking at probably probably 80% of the oil is new every day, but every Wednesday 100% of it is brand new. >> When you heat seed oils, you get major problems. So when you heat the oils here, you make acryine. Acrylene is also found in cigarette smoke. It's a known carcinogen. And the amounts found in a large French fry of acryine are equivalent to a whole pack of cigarettes. That's another major problem with seed oils, guys, is that the majority of seed oils are used in cooking and heating, and that creates all these byproducts, which are even worse for you. We did not brave the long

chunks.title: Health Risks of In-N-Out Menu Items
chunks.summary: The host breaks down the nutritional pitfalls of specific items like the French fries and secret sauce, explaining their ingredients. He contrasts these poor choices with healthier alternatives available from the menu.
chunks.transcript: line in the drive-thru, and this is a very popular lunch destination. I mean, it's 3:00 in the afternoon on a Monday. It's popping. So, what are the worst things you can get at In-N-Out? Basically, let's start with the French fries. We talked about that. They're cooked in sunflower oil. It's changed somewhat every day and then once a week. So, this sunflower oil these are cooked in certainly has high levels of acryine, a compound found in cigarettes. Basically, the fries are pretty toxic for humans. I got a double double burger, which is what most people probably get. And the beef here is 100% beef, so that's good. And the cheese is good. But then they put a sauce on it. And this is the secret sauce. They now tell you the ingredients. I've called them in the past to try and get the ingredients. They kept me on hold for a long time. Anyway, 12 ingredients in the sauce. Seed oils, processed sugar, vinegar, that kind of stuff. The biggest thing is that it has processed sugar and seed oils in the sauce. It's like the equivalent of the Big Mac secret sauce, but you can basically guess that any fast food restaurant that has a secret sauce is just going to combine ketchup, sugar, and seed oils. That's what makes all these up. So, you're getting extra seed oils on the burger with that. And if that weren't enough, what always is wild to me are the buns at these places. You can see this bun is basically just like leaking oil. There's seed oils coming out of the bun. So, what's in the bun? 18 different ingredients, preservatives, you got dough conditioners, you got seed oils, you got processed sugar, you got flour enriched with artificial vitamins like folic acid, which is not great because folic acid can block actual folate receptors in humans. That's a whole separate story. If you were really going to make a good bun, you would just make it from sourdough, right? How do you make sourdough bread? It should be three ingredients: water, flour, and yeast. This bun has 18 ingredients. You already know your great-g grandandmother wouldn't recognize a bunch of the ingredients in this bun. These are things our ancestors have never ever eaten and we just don't know how they affect us as humans long term. So, we get back to what I think of as the remembering and food for humans. If you want to be healthy, look for single ingredient food, things like beef, apples, oranges. You get the idea. If you're going to eat something with a label, would your great-grandmother recognize everything on that label? Your great-grandmother doesn't have any idea what most of what's in this bun is, you don't want to eat it. It's not food for humans. And longterm, this is the type of stuff that slowly in a sneaky creepy fashion actually causes metabolic dysfunction and obesity and issues in humans. The less of this kind of stuff you eat, the better you do. The more that you eat, the worse you do. That's all. That's the simple truth. Even if you try and restrict calories, it's going to be very hard because a lot of the ingredients in these foods make you hungrier. So anyway, you get the idea.

chunks.title: Dinner at Texas Roadhouse
chunks.summary: The video concludes with a visit to Texas Roadhouse, where the host critiques the use of soybean oil in their honey butter and the problematic ingredients in their bread and appetizers. He discusses potential healthier options with staff and the importance of real butter for cooking.
chunks.transcript: most popular chain steakhouse in the country. And you're not going to believe what's actually in their butter. Let's go. All right, so it's time to grade In-N-Out. Even sitting down at the table, we got trouble in front of us. Let's see if we can navigate this menu and I'll see what the best thing on the menu is and the worst thing on the menu. We're going to start with the cactus blossom, but let's make it a fire blossom. Then we're going to get just one steak. I'm going to get the 8 oz New York. But I'm curious, I have food sensitivities. What is it cooked in? How is it cooked? Is it cooked in oil? What oil is it cooked in? >> Um, I'm pretty sure it is oil. I don't know about oil, but I have to ask the kitchen just to >> Can you check? It's a type of butter. >> No, no, margarine is not a type of butter. Margarine is not the same thing as butter. It's totally different. Margarine is made from like soybean oil. So, can they cook it in butter? >> Okay. [laughter] See, the kitchen at Texas Roadhouse thinks margarine is a type of butter. No. Why? Who cooks a steak in margarine? That's a sacrilege, guys. >> Okay. Yes, we could do it in regular butter. >> You can do it in real butter. So, let's do an 8 oz New York cooked in real butter. But you guys usually put like seasoning [music] on the steak, right? I just want a plain steak. They just take it out of the case and you can put salt on it, but nothing else. And then put it on like in butter. That's perfect because I'm sensitive to like MSG and oils. >> Okay. Gotcha. >> Yeah. To undo all the good stuff, we'll do the fire blossom and that's it for us. Oh, yeah. Yeah. Can I get steamed vegetables with my steak? >> That's all. >> And does someone choose you want double vegetables? >> Yeah. Can I get a plain baked potato? Yeah. Okay. So, here's an important lesson. At both IHOP and a Texas Roadhouse, if you ask them what it's cooked in, you usually find that it's cooked in seed oils. And if you ask them to cook it in something else, like real butter, they will do it. But it's important to also understand that steakouses like to put seasonings on their steak. I did a little research before we came here and it turns out that the seasoning the Texas Roadhouse uses on their steak contains, you're killing me, soybean oil and MSG. So, you have to ask, I want a steak with salt, no seasoning, cooked in butter. And then we got the worst thing on the [music] menu, which we'll show you guys in a second. Okay, so we got the best thing at Texas Roadhouse and the worst thing at Texas Roadhouse. Let's start with the bad news. This is the fire blossom. Over 3,200 calories. It is an onion battered in some sort of a wheat batter dipped and fried in soybean oil. It's dusted with some paprika and then you've got a cheese sauce in the middle that's over 1,000 calories. So, this is super popular. I asked the waitress what the most popular thing on the menu was. This the most popular thing on the menu has over 3200 calories of deep fried seed oils and cheese sauce. And I don't even know what's in the cheese sauce. So, my point here is that when you come to a place like this, you could get appetizers like this that are popular that are horrible for you. But here's the good news. As you saw when I was ordering, if you don't ask them at Texas Roadhouse, they're going to cook your steak in seed oils and they're going to season your steak with seed oils and MSG. But it took a few back and forths with the kitchen. They say that they cooked my steak in butter. All they put on it was salt. And then as sides, I got some steamed vegetables. I'm not going to eat those. Those are for rabbits. But if you wanted to, you could eat steamed vegetables. [music] And you got a baked potato. That's pretty cool. So, there is good food here, but like all of these chain restaurants we've seen through this whole day of eating, for an average American, if you just do what's easy, you're going to end up with garbage. And there's good news. If you're intentional about the way you're eating and you're careful, you can get decent food even when you're on the go. As you guys know, I cook 99% of my meals at home. I'm always at the grocery store. I'm cooking my own food. But every once in a while, I'm traveling. And I do these sorts of things when I'm traveling. If I go to restaurants like this, I'm going to ask them, "What is the food cooked in? Can you cook it in butter?" And almost every restaurant that I go to has butter. Very few restaurants have no butter. Our sweet waitress [music] thought that margarine was a type of butter. We corrected her with this. So, we've educated. Margarine is not a type of butter. Margarine is a highly processed, industrially refined seed oil product.

chunks.title: Final Thoughts and Healthier Eating Strategies
chunks.summary: The host summarizes the experiences from the day, offering insights on making healthier choices even at chain restaurants. He emphasizes the importance of questioning cooking methods and ingredient sources to maintain healthier eating habits.
chunks.transcript: butter. And most restaurants, they want to cook in a way that you like. If you just tell them the secret, hey, I have food sensitivities. I have allergies to seed oils. I have allergies to MSG. They're going to be careful. And most of them will cook in butter for you. So, if you're intentional about the way you're eating, you can make a day of eating as an average American, even at fast food places, much better than it could have been. At IHOP, I got four eggs cooked sunnyside up, cooked in butter. At In-N-Out, I got a Flying Dutchman. And here at Texas Roadhouse, I got a New York steak cooked in butter with a baked potato and broccoli and carrots. Those are all pretty reasonably healthy foods. You could eat those foods longterm and not gain weight. In fact, I would argue that you could eat things that I ordered at every single restaurant today, which are chain restaurants in America, and actually lose weight. These are way healthier than what the average American eats. So, [music] the choice is yours. Do you want to eat poor food, or do you want to eat high quality food? With a little bit of education, which hopefully I provided for you guys, and a little bit of intention, you can turn food like this into choices that look like this, and you'll be much better off long term. If you want to learn more about the way that I shop for food and prepare food, and what I eat on a daily basis, there's a simple shopping and food preparation guide on my website that shows you how I shop, what I eat, and how much I eat in a day. So, my website is paulsaladinomd.com. [music] You can go there for the free guide. I'll put a link directly to the guide in the description of this video. All right, guys. That's it for this video. We'll probably eat this steak. And [music] thanks for watching this video. Please send it to someone you know who eats at chain restaurants or who needs to hear this information. The way that we get healthier as a community is by sharing videos like this with people who need to know this. Maybe it's your parents, maybe it's your brother, your sister, or your grandma who needs to know how to eat more healthfully at chain restaurants. A day of eating as an average American can be a nightmare or it can actually be pretty good. Your choice. I'll see you guys in the next
